news_summary: Innovative advancements in sustainable energy and environmental awareness are driving transformative changes in technology and policy to better protect the planet.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées innovantes en matière d'énergie durable et de sensibilisation à l'environnement sont à l'origine de changements profonds dans les domaines technologiques et politiques, visant à mieux protéger la planète.
news_summary_es: Los avances innovadores en materia de energía sostenible y concienciación medioambiental están impulsando cambios transformadores en la tecnología y las políticas para proteger mejor el planeta.

articles.title: Scientists develop dirt-powered fuel cell that could replace batteries
articles.summary: Scientists have developed a fuel cell that uses microbes in soil to produce electricity. The device can power underground sensors for tasks like monitoring moisture or detecting touch, without needing batteries or solar panels. It works in both dry and wet conditions and even lasts longer than similar technologies. This could pave the way for sustainable, low-maintenance sensors in farming and environmental monitoring.
articles.content:
Researchers led by Northwestern University have developed a fuel cell that generates electricity using microbes naturally found in soil. The device, roughly the size of a paperback book, produces small amounts of power by capturing energy released as these microorganisms break down organic material in dirt.

This soil-powered system is designed to run underground sensors used in precision agriculture and environmental monitoring. It offers a potential alternative to traditional batteries, which contain toxic and flammable materials, rely on complex global supply chains, and contribute to growing electronic waste.

Powering Sensors Without Batteries

To demonstrate its capabilities, the team used the fuel cell to operate sensors that measure soil moisture and detect touch. This touch-sensing ability could help monitor wildlife movement, such as animals passing through a field. The system also includes a small antenna that sends data wirelessly by reflecting existing radio frequency signals, which keeps energy use extremely low.

The device proved reliable across a wide range of conditions. It functioned in both dry soil and flooded environments, and it produced more sustained power than similar systems, lasting about 120% longer.

The study was published in the Proceedings of the Association for Computing Machinery on Interactive, Mobile, Wearable and Ubiquitous Technologies. The researchers also released their designs, tutorials and simulation tools publicly so others can build on the work.

Why Soil Microbes Matter for the Internet of Things

"The number of devices in the Internet of Things (IoT) is constantly growing," said Northwestern alumnus Bill Yen, who led the work. "If we imagine a future with trillions of these devices, we cannot build every one of them out of lithium, heavy metals and toxins that are dangerous to the environment. We need to find alternatives that can provide low amounts of energy to power a decentralized network of devices. In a search for solutions, we looked to soil microbial fuel cells, which use special microbes to break down soil and use that low amount of energy to power sensors. As long as there is organic carbon in the soil for the microbes to break down, the fuel cell can potentially last forever."

Microbial fuel cells, often called MFCs, work somewhat like a battery. They include an anode, cathode and electrolyte, but instead of chemical reactions, they rely on bacteria that naturally release electrons. When these electrons move through the system, they create an electric current.

"These microbes are ubiquitous; they already live in soil everywhere," said Northwestern's George Wells, a senior author on the study. "We can use very simple engineered systems to capture their electricity. We're not going to power entire cities with this energy. But we can capture minute amounts of energy to fuel practical, low-power applications."

Challenges With Solar and Battery-Powered Sensors

Precision agriculture depends on large networks of sensors that continuously track soil conditions such as moisture, nutrients and contaminants. These data help farmers make more informed decisions and improve crop yields.

But powering those sensors is a major challenge. Batteries eventually run out and must be replaced, which is impractical across large farms. Solar panels can also be unreliable because they become dirty, require sunlight and take up space.

"If you want to put a sensor out in the wild, in a farm or in a wetland, you are constrained to putting a battery in it or harvesting solar energy," Yen said. "Solar panels don't work well in dirty environments because they get covered with dirt, do not work when the sun isn't out and take up a lot of space. Batteries also are challenging because they run out of power. Farmers are not going to go around a 100-acre farm to regularly swap out batteries or dust off solar panels."

The researchers instead focused on harvesting energy directly from the soil itself, turning the environment into the power source.

Why Earlier Microbial Fuel Cells Fell Short

Soil-based microbial fuel cells have existed since 1911, but they have struggled to deliver consistent performance. These systems need both moisture and oxygen to function properly, which can be difficult to maintain underground, especially in dry conditions.

"Although MFCs have existed as a concept for more than a century, their unreliable performance and low output power have stymied efforts to make practical use of them, especially in low-moisture conditions," Yen said.

A New Design Improves Performance

To address these issues, the team spent two years developing and testing different designs. They compared four versions and collected nine months of performance data before selecting a final prototype, which they tested outdoors.

The breakthrough came from a change in geometry. Instead of placing the anode and cathode parallel to each other, the new design positions them perpendicular.

The anode, made of carbon felt (an inexpensive, abundant conductor to capture the microbes' electrons), lies horizontally beneath the soil. The cathode, made of a conductive metal, extends vertically to the surface.

This structure helps solve several problems at once. The top of the device remains exposed to air, ensuring a steady oxygen supply. At the same time, the lower portion stays buried in moist soil, maintaining hydration even during dry conditions. A protective cap prevents debris from entering, while a small air chamber allows airflow.

The design also improves resilience during flooding. A waterproof coating allows the cathode to keep functioning, and the vertical layout helps it dry gradually after water recedes.

Strong Results in Real-World Conditions

The final prototype performed well across a wide range of soil conditions, from moderately dry soil (41% water by volume) to fully submerged environments. On average, it generated 68 times more power than required to run its sensors.

These results suggest the system is robust enough for real-world deployment in agricultural fields or natural environments.

Ongoing Research and Future Potential

Since the study was first published, interest in microbial fuel cells has continued to grow. Researchers are working to improve efficiency, stability and materials, including exploring biodegradable designs that could further reduce environmental impact.

The Northwestern team notes that all parts of their system can be sourced from common hardware materials. They are now aiming to create fully biodegradable versions that avoid complex supply chains and conflict minerals.

"With the COVID-19 pandemic, we all became familiar with how a crisis can disrupt the global supply chain for electronics," said study co-author Josiah Hester, a former Northwestern faculty member who is now at the Georgia Institute of Technology. "We want to build devices that use local supply chains and low-cost materials so that computing is accessible for all communities."

While the technology is not intended to power large systems, it could play an important role in supporting low-energy devices across agriculture, environmental monitoring and the expanding Internet of Things.

Key Points

Scientists have created a new fuel cell that uses naturally occurring soil microbes to generate electricity

The system can power underground sensors that track soil moisture and even detect movement or touch

It continues working in a wide range of conditions, from dry soil to fully flooded environments

This technology could offer a cleaner alternative to batteries for sensors used in precision agriculture

The study, "Soil-powered computing: The engineer's guide to practical soil microbial fuel cell design," was supported by the National Science Foundation (award number CNS-2038853), the Agricultural and Food Research Initiative (award number 2023-67021-40628) from the USDA National Institute of Food and Agriculture, the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation, VMware Research and 3M.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers developed a sustainable, dirt-powered fuel cell that can replace batteries for underground sensors. This innovation has broad implications for precision agriculture and environmental monitoring by providing a low-maintenance, eco-friendly power source that works in diverse soil conditions. The technology addresses major challenges with current battery and solar-powered sensors, reduces electronic waste, and supports scalable IoT applications with local, low-cost materials. The story is well-detailed, focused on a single impactful advancement with clear environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create soil-powered fuel cell to run underground sensors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists want better ways to power small sensors that monitor soil and the environment. Current options like batteries and solar panels can be costly, harmful to nature, or unreliable, especially underground.

**What happened** – A team at Northwestern University developed a new fuel cell that uses microbes naturally found in soil to create electricity. This device, about the size of a paperback book, powers sensors that measure soil moisture and detect touch. It works underground in dry or wet soil and lasts longer than past versions. The design positions parts of the cell both underground and above ground to keep oxygen flowing and maintain moisture.

**Impact** – This fuel cell offers a cleaner and more reliable power source for sensors used in farming and environmental monitoring. It avoids toxic materials found in batteries, reduces electronic waste, and solves problems with solar panels like dirt coverage and lack of sunlight. Because it uses common materials and soil microbes, it can keep running as long as the soil has organic matter.

**What's next step** – Researchers are working to make the fuel cell even better, including versions that are fully biodegradable. They hope this technology will support many small devices in agriculture and nature, helping farmers and scientists track conditions without frequent maintenance or harmful waste.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By harnessing electricity from soil microbes, scientists have created a durable, eco-friendly fuel cell that can power underground sensors without batteries or solar panels.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une pile à combustible alimentée par le sol pour capteurs souterrains
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent de meilleures façons d’alimenter de petits capteurs qui surveillent le sol et l’environnement. Les options actuelles comme les piles et les panneaux solaires sont coûteuses, nuisibles à la nature ou peu fiables, surtout sous terre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de l’Université Northwestern a développé une nouvelle pile à combustible qui utilise des microbes naturellement présents dans le sol pour produire de l’électricité. Cet appareil, de la taille d’un livre de poche, alimente des capteurs qui mesurent l’humidité du sol et détectent le contact. Il fonctionne sous terre dans un sol sec ou humide et dure plus longtemps que les versions précédentes. Le design place des parties de la pile à la fois sous terre et en surface pour assurer l’oxygène et garder l’humidité.

**Impact** – Cette pile à combustible offre une source d’énergie plus propre et fiable pour les capteurs utilisés en agriculture et en surveillance de l’environnement. Elle évite les matériaux toxiques des piles, réduit les déchets électroniques et règle les problèmes des panneaux solaires comme la saleté ou le manque de soleil. Grâce à ses matériaux communs et aux microbes du sol, elle peut fonctionner tant que le sol contient de la matière organique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs travaillent à améliorer encore la pile, notamment en créant des versions totalement biodégradables. Ils espèrent que cette technologie soutiendra de nombreux petits appareils en agriculture et dans la nature, aidant les agriculteurs et les scientifiques à suivre les conditions sans entretien fréquent ni déchets nocifs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En exploitant l’électricité des microbes du sol, des scientifiques ont créé une pile à combustible durable et écologique pour alimenter des capteurs souterrains sans piles ni panneaux solaires.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean una pila de combustible que usa tierra para alimentar sensores subterráneos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan mejores formas de alimentar pequeños sensores que monitorean el suelo y el medio ambiente. Las opciones actuales como baterías y paneles solares pueden ser costosas, dañinas para la naturaleza o poco confiables, especialmente bajo tierra.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de la Universidad Northwestern desarrolló una nueva pila de combustible que utiliza microbios que se encuentran naturalmente en el suelo para generar electricidad. Este dispositivo, del tamaño de un libro de bolsillo, alimenta sensores que miden la humedad del suelo y detectan el tacto. Funciona bajo tierra en suelos secos o húmedos y dura más que versiones anteriores. El diseño coloca partes de la pila tanto bajo tierra como en la superficie para mantener el oxígeno y la humedad.

**Impacto** – Esta pila ofrece una fuente de energía más limpia y confiable para sensores usados en agricultura y monitoreo ambiental. Evita materiales tóxicos de las baterías, reduce residuos electrónicos y soluciona problemas de los paneles solares como la suciedad o la falta de sol. Usando materiales comunes y microbios del suelo, puede funcionar mientras haya materia orgánica.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores trabajan para mejorar la pila, incluyendo versiones completamente biodegradables. Esperan que esta tecnología apoye muchos dispositivos pequeños en agricultura y naturaleza, ayudando a agricultores y científicos a monitorear sin mantenimiento frecuente ni residuos dañinos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Aprovechando la electricidad de los microbios del suelo, científicos crearon una pila de combustible duradera y ecológica para alimentar sensores subterráneos sin baterías ni paneles solares.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a small, book-sized device partially buried in rich soil, with a vertical metal rod reaching above ground surrounded by tiny glowing microbes symbolized as gentle, luminous orbs emitting soft light, powering delicate underground sensors depicted as subtle glowing nodes connected by thin lines, set within a natural farm field landscape under soft natural colors.

articles.title: A new force of nature is reshaping the planet, study finds
articles.summary: Human societies didn’t just adapt to the planet—they learned to reshape it. From early fire use to today’s global supply chains, our cultural and social innovations have unlocked extraordinary power to transform Earth and improve human life. But that progress has come with serious costs, including climate change, pollution, and mass extinction. Instead of framing this era—the Anthropocene—as pure crisis, Erle Ellis argues it’s also proof of something hopeful: when people work together, they can drive massive positive change.
articles.content:
Human societies have not just adapted to the natural world. They have steadily learned how to transform it. Drawing on research from archaeology, ecology, anthropology, and evolutionary theory, Erle Ellis, professor of geography and environmental systems at the University of Maryland Baltimore County, explains how cultural practices have evolved to give humans extraordinary influence over the ecosystems that sustain them.

From early uses of fire to cook food and shape landscapes to modern systems like industrial agriculture, global trade, and rapidly growing cities, societies have developed powerful tools and institutions. These social and cultural advances have allowed humans to reshape the planet on a massive scale while improving their ability to survive and thrive.

Understanding the Anthropocene and Human Impact

Ellis is a leading researcher studying the Anthropocene, the current geological age defined by the large-scale impact of human activity on Earth. He leads the Anthroecology Lab, which examines how human societies interact with ecosystems at every level, from local environments to the entire planet. His work focuses on how these relationships can be guided toward more sustainable outcomes.

In recent years, the concept of the Anthropocene has gained even broader attention across science and policy discussions. Ongoing research continues to reinforce the idea that human activity is now one of the dominant forces shaping Earth's systems, from climate patterns to biodiversity.

Progress for People, Costs for the Planet

Human innovation has brought major gains in health, longevity, and quality of life. At the same time, these advances have come with serious environmental costs. Climate change, species extinctions, and widespread pollution are all linked to the ways human societies have expanded and intensified their use of natural resources.

These challenges highlight the need for action. A better future depends on addressing environmental damage while maintaining the benefits that human progress has made possible.

Beyond Crisis Thinking Toward Collective Action

Ellis argues that focusing only on environmental crisis can miss a key point. The same collective abilities that allowed societies to transform the planet can also be used to improve it. History shows that when people cooperate, they can solve complex problems and reshape their surroundings in positive ways.

Rather than relying solely on narratives of limits or collapse, long-term solutions may depend on tapping into shared goals and collective ambition. Recent research continues to support this perspective, emphasizing that social cooperation and cultural change are essential for addressing global environmental challenges.

The Power of Social and Cultural Systems

Ellis also highlights the limits of relying only on natural sciences to predict and manage the rapid changes seen in the Anthropocene. While scientific data is critical, it is social and cultural systems that have consistently enabled societies to adapt and succeed.

Institutions, shared values, and collective decision-making play a central role in shaping outcomes. These same systems will be crucial in building more sustainable relationships with the natural world.

If a better future is to be achieved, these capabilities must extend beyond human societies to include the broader web of life.

Reconnecting People and Nature

"Re-emphasizing the kinship relationships among all living beings -- our common evolutionary ancestry -- is a start, combined with new ways to connect people and nature, from remote sensing to webcams, to nature apps, to community conservation reserves, corridor networks, and ecotourism," shares Ellis. "Aspirations for a better future must also make peace with the past through restoration of Indigenous and traditional sovereignty over lands and waters."

This perspective aligns with growing global efforts to restore ecosystems, support Indigenous stewardship, and use technology to strengthen connections between people and nature.

A Future Shaped by Human Potential

Ellis stresses that the ability to create a more sustainable and equitable future is not new. The tools, knowledge, and social systems needed to drive change have existed for decades. What is often missing is widespread recognition and motivation to act.

The challenge now is to turn awareness into action. By recognizing the scale of human influence and embracing shared aspirations for a better world, societies can begin to use their collective power to shape a more positive future for both people and the planet.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant positive potential of human cultural and social innovation to reshape the planet sustainably, emphasizing collective action and cooperation to address environmental challenges. It presents a hopeful perspective on the Anthropocene, focusing on broad societal impact and the possibility of large-scale environmental restoration and sustainability.
articles.personality_title: Humans have reshaped Earth before — now they can do it again for the better
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Humans have changed the Earth for thousands of years, from using fire to build cities and farms. This long history shows people can shape nature, but it has also caused problems like pollution and climate change.

**What happened** – Erle Ellis, a professor studying how humans impact the planet, explains that our cultural ideas and teamwork gave us the power to change ecosystems worldwide. His research looks at how societies can use this power not just to harm, but to improve the environment.

**Impact** – This view is important because it shows the Anthropocene—the age where humans strongly affect Earth—is not only about damage. It reveals that when people work together, they can fix problems and create better places to live. Ellis points out social systems and shared values are key tools for positive change.

**What's next step** – Moving forward, people need to turn understanding into action by cooperating on big projects like restoring nature and supporting Indigenous land care. Technology and community efforts can help connect people with nature and guide sustainable choices.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Human teamwork and culture have shaped Earth’s past and now hold the key to building a healthier planet for the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les humains ont déjà transformé la Terre — ils peuvent le refaire pour le mieux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les humains changent la Terre depuis des milliers d’années, en utilisant le feu, en construisant des villes et des fermes. Cette histoire montre que les humains peuvent façonner la nature, mais cela a aussi créé des problèmes comme la pollution et le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Erle Ellis, un professeur qui étudie l’impact humain sur la planète, explique que nos idées culturelles et notre travail en équipe nous ont donné le pouvoir de changer les écosystèmes dans le monde entier. Ses recherches montrent comment les sociétés peuvent utiliser ce pouvoir non seulement pour nuire, mais pour améliorer l’environnement.

**Impact** – Cette idée est importante car elle montre que l’Anthropocène — l’époque où l’humain influence fortement la Terre — n’est pas seulement une période de dégâts. Elle révèle que lorsque les gens travaillent ensemble, ils peuvent résoudre des problèmes et créer de meilleurs endroits où vivre. Ellis souligne que les systèmes sociaux et les valeurs partagées sont des outils clés pour un changement positif.

**Prochaine étape** – Pour l’avenir, les gens doivent transformer cette compréhension en actions en coopérant sur de grands projets comme la restauration de la nature et le soutien à la gestion des terres par les peuples autochtones. La technologie et les efforts communautaires peuvent aider à rapprocher les humains de la nature et guider des choix durables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le travail d’équipe et la culture humaine ont façonné la Terre autrefois et sont aujourd’hui la clé pour construire une planète plus saine.
articles.personality_title_es: Los humanos ya transformaron la Tierra — ahora pueden hacerlo de nuevo para mejorarla
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los humanos han cambiado la Tierra durante miles de años, usando el fuego y construyendo ciudades y granjas. Esta historia muestra que las personas pueden moldear la naturaleza, pero también ha causado problemas como contaminación y cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – Erle Ellis, un profesor que estudia cómo los humanos afectan el planeta, explica que nuestras ideas culturales y el trabajo en equipo nos dieron el poder para cambiar ecosistemas en todo el mundo. Su investigación muestra cómo las sociedades pueden usar este poder no solo para dañar, sino para mejorar el medio ambiente.

**Impacto** – Esta idea es importante porque muestra que el Antropoceno — la era en que los humanos influyen mucho en la Tierra — no es solo una época de daño. Revela que cuando las personas trabajan juntas, pueden resolver problemas y crear mejores lugares para vivir. Ellis destaca que los sistemas sociales y los valores compartidos son herramientas clave para el cambio positivo.

**Próximo paso** – En el futuro, las personas deben convertir este entendimiento en acción cooperando en grandes proyectos como restaurar la naturaleza y apoyar el cuidado de tierras indígenas. La tecnología y los esfuerzos comunitarios pueden ayudar a conectar a la gente con la naturaleza y guiar elecciones sostenibles.

**Frase clave** – El trabajo en equipo y la cultura humana moldearon la Tierra antes y ahora son la clave para construir un planeta más saludable.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vast, interconnected landscape where glowing, intricate networks of roots and branches weave between ancient fire pits, terraced fields, bustling city silhouettes, and flowing rivers, symbolizing the evolving human influence and cultural cooperation shaping Earth’s ecosystems in harmony with nature.

articles.title: Maine presses pause on large data centers. Will other states follow its lead?
articles.content:
This story was originally published by Inside Climate News and is reproduced here as part of the Climate Desk collaboration.

Maine is now the first state to pass a moratorium on the development of large data centers, and others may follow.

The Maine House and Senate this week passed LD 307, which prohibits state and local governments from approving data centers with at least 20 megawatts of electricity demand until at least October 2027. The bill awaits a signature from Governor Janet Mills, who has not commented on whether she will sign it.

Maine is one of about a dozen states with legislative proposals this year to pause or ban data centers amid rising concerns about the projects’ size and energy and water consumption. Community opponents of data centers are grappling with what they view as intrusive development as the broader public worries that the rise of artificial intelligence — fueled by data centers — will lead to mass job losses.

Analysts who follow state-level discussions of data centers say Minnesota is also a good candidate to pass a bill on this subject, along with Illinois, even though there is not yet a bill pending in Illinois.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

U.S. data centers had more than 50 gigawatts of electricity demand as of last year, which, for context, is about double the peak demand of the entire New England grid, which covers six states, including Maine. And this is before some supersized data centers that are still in early planning stages. It’s not clear how the electricity grid will build enough power plants to meet demand, or how residential ratepayers can be shielded from high electricity bills driven by data center demand.

The debate in Maine gives a sense of how this topic interacts with partisan politics. It passed the House, 79-62, and the Senate, 21-13.

The Democrats who control the House and Senate described the bill as providing breathing room to write rules to regulate data centers. The opponents, mainly Republicans, said the bill would discourage investment and harm the economy.

The measure calls for convening a special council to evaluate concerns about data centers and recommend new policies to the legislature.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Maine has had relatively little data center development, with about 10 sites, none of which are large “hyperscalers” that are inspiring backlash in Virginia, Texas, and other states.

The goal of the bill is to use the experiences in other states as “a cautionary tale that Maine could really learn from,” state Representative Melanie Sachs, a Democrat and lead sponsor of the bill, said in an interview.

“Let’s just make sure our regulatory framework can meet the moment,” she said.

State Senator Matt Harrington, a Republican, was among the opponents. He said the bill would delay or cancel major projects, including data centers being discussed in the communities of Sanford and Jay.

“This is billions of dollars in potential investment in Maine,” he said during debate last week.

Mills had indicated she wanted the bill to include an exemption for the project in Jay, which would redevelop a former paper mill site. The bill does not contain such an exemption.

The governor could sign the legislation, veto it, or allow it to become law by taking no action within 10 days. Sachs said she doesn’t know what Mills will do, and her office did not respond to a request for comment.

Environmental advocates are praising the bill as a model for how states can take control of a wave of development that has outpaced regulators’ ability to protect the public.

“Voters do not want these facilities in their backyard,” said Sarah Woodbury, legislative director for Maine Conservation Voters, an environmental advocacy group. “In Maine, every time a community has tried to get [a data center], the town has rebelled and it has failed. So I think the politics around data centers are similar here [as] they are in other places.”

Woodbury is proud that Maine would be leading the country in reining in data centers if the bill becomes law.

Lawmakers in 13 other states have introduced bills or resolutions that would pause development of data centers in some way, but none of these have passed a legislative chamber, according to the North Carolina Clean Energy Technology Center.

Dozens of local governments across the country have also adopted bans and moratoria, and many others are in the works.

U.S. Senator Bernie Sanders, an independent from Vermont; and U.S. Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, a Democrat from New York, have proposed a national moratorium on AI data centers.

“The politics of this are still evolving,” said Anthony Elmo, a researcher for Good Jobs First, a nonprofit watchdog organization that tracks government subsidies for corporations.

Elmo cautions against characterizing data centers as a partisan topic. He has seen opposition to development from Democrats and Republicans, especially when talking about specific projects.

That said, he thinks data center restrictions are most likely in states where Democrats control the legislature and the governor’s office, in part because Republicans tend to be skeptical of regulation.

Asked which state is most likely to follow Maine, he said there is no clear answer, but he is watching Illinois and Minnesota as examples of places where a data center backlash is happening and elected officials are sympathetic to the concerns.

Regardless of what states do, he expects data center backlash to grow as the developments get bigger, expand to more places, and their backers continue to have difficulty answering basic questions from residents.
articles.reasoning: Maine's legislative moratorium on large data centers addresses significant environmental concerns about energy and water consumption, setting a precedent for other states. This pause allows for regulatory frameworks to catch up with rapid data center growth, potentially reducing environmental impact and protecting public resources. The story has broad significance as data centers' energy demands affect regional grids and communities nationwide, and it includes detailed context about the bill, political debate, and potential wider adoption.
articles.personality_title: Maine pauses large data centers to protect resources and plan better rules
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Data centers are buildings that store and process huge amounts of digital information. They use a lot of electricity and water, which has raised concerns in many states as these centers grow bigger and more common.

**What happened** – Maine’s lawmakers passed a bill called LD 307 that stops approval of new large data centers using 20 megawatts or more of electricity until October 2027. This pause gives the state time to create better rules about where and how these centers can be built. The bill now waits for Governor Janet Mills to decide whether to sign it into law.

**Impact** – Maine is the first state to officially pause large data center projects, responding to worries about their heavy use of energy and water. This is important because the total electricity used by data centers in the U.S. is already very high—about double the peak electricity use of the entire six-state New England region. Maine’s decision could inspire other states like Minnesota and Illinois to take similar steps. The bill also shows how communities and politicians are thinking about balancing new technology with protecting resources and local interests.

**What’s next step** – A special council will be formed to study data centers and suggest new policies to lawmakers. Meanwhile, other states are watching Maine’s move closely, and some have introduced similar ideas. The governor’s choice to sign or reject the bill will shape how quickly these changes happen in Maine.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Maine’s temporary halt on large data centers sets a new example for managing their growth carefully to protect energy and water resources.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Maine suspend les grands centres de données pour mieux protéger les ressources
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les centres de données sont des bâtiments qui stockent et traitent de grandes quantités d’informations numériques. Ils consomment beaucoup d’électricité et d’eau, ce qui inquiète plusieurs États à mesure que ces centres deviennent plus grands et plus nombreux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les législateurs du Maine ont adopté un projet de loi, LD 307, qui suspend l’approbation des nouveaux grands centres de données utilisant 20 mégawatts ou plus d’électricité jusqu’en octobre 2027. Cette pause donne au Maine le temps d’élaborer de meilleures règles sur l’emplacement et la construction de ces centres. Le projet de loi attend maintenant la signature du gouverneur Janet Mills.

**Impact** – Le Maine est le premier État à suspendre officiellement les grands projets de centres de données, en réponse aux inquiétudes liées à leur forte consommation d’énergie et d’eau. C’est important car la consommation totale d’électricité des centres de données aux États-Unis est déjà très élevée, environ deux fois la consommation maximale de la région du New England qui regroupe six États. Cette décision pourrait encourager d’autres États comme le Minnesota et l’Illinois à agir de même. Le projet montre aussi comment les communautés et les élus réfléchissent à l’équilibre entre technologie et protection des ressources.

**Prochaine étape** – Un conseil spécial sera créé pour étudier les centres de données et proposer de nouvelles politiques aux législateurs. D’autres États observent de près la décision du Maine et certains ont présenté des idées similaires. La décision du gouverneur de signer ou non la loi influencera la rapidité de ces changements dans le Maine.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La suspension temporaire des grands centres de données par le Maine montre un nouvel exemple de gestion prudente de leur développement pour protéger l’énergie et l’eau.
articles.personality_title_es: Maine detiene grandes centros de datos para proteger recursos y planificar mejores reglas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los centros de datos son edificios que almacenan y procesan grandes cantidades de información digital. Usan mucha electricidad y agua, lo que ha generado preocupaciones en varios estados a medida que estos centros crecen y se vuelven más comunes.

**Qué pasó** – Los legisladores de Maine aprobaron un proyecto de ley llamado LD 307 que detiene la aprobación de nuevos centros de datos grandes que usen 20 megavatios o más de electricidad hasta octubre de 2027. Esta pausa da tiempo al estado para crear mejores reglas sobre dónde y cómo se pueden construir estos centros. El proyecto de ley ahora espera que la gobernadora Janet Mills decida si lo firma para convertirlo en ley.

**Impacto** – Maine es el primer estado que pausa oficialmente los proyectos de grandes centros de datos, respondiendo a preocupaciones sobre su alto consumo de energía y agua. Esto es importante porque la electricidad que usan los centros de datos en EE.UU. ya es muy alta, aproximadamente el doble del pico de consumo eléctrico de toda la región de Nueva Inglaterra, que incluye seis estados. La decisión de Maine podría inspirar a otros estados como Minnesota e Illinois a tomar medidas similares. La ley también muestra cómo las comunidades y políticos piensan en equilibrar la nueva tecnología con la protección de recursos y los intereses locales.

**Próximo paso** – Se formará un consejo especial para estudiar los centros de datos y recomendar nuevas políticas a los legisladores. Mientras tanto, otros estados observan de cerca la decisión de Maine, y algunos han propuesto ideas similares. La decisión de la gobernadora de firmar o rechazar la ley determinará qué tan rápido ocurren estos cambios en Maine.

**Resumen en una frase** – La pausa temporal de Maine en los grandes centros de datos marca un nuevo ejemplo para manejar cuidadosamente su crecimiento y proteger los recursos de energía y agua.
articles.image_prompt: A serene Maine landscape featuring a stylized electrical grid gently paused by a symbolic hand-shaped cloud, with softly glowing data center server towers subtly integrated into the hillsides, surrounded by calm rivers and small towns under a warm, pastel sky.
